Thus, in general terms, it is a power which cannot be exercised until the ‘tribunal which has power ... is called upon to take action’ (Huddart Parker), which (subject to limited exceptions) proceeds by way of open and public inquiry, which involves the application of the rules of natural justice, and which is directed to ascertaining ‘the law as it is and the facts as they are, followed by an application of the law as determined to the facts as determined’: Tasmanian Breweries, per Kitto J.” (references omitted);

more recently, Gaudron J explained the nature of judicial power in Nicholas v The Queen in these terms:

“In my view, consistency with the essential character of a court and with the nature of judicial power necessitates that a court not be required or authorised to proceed in a manner that does not ensure equality before the law, impartiality and the appearance of impartiality, the right of a party to meet the case made against him or her, the independent determination of the matter in controversy by application of the law to facts determined in accordance with the rules and procedures which truly permit the facts to be ascertained and, in the case of criminal proceedings, the determination of guilt or innocence by means of a fair trial according to the law. It means, moreover, that a court cannot be required or authorised to proceed in any manner which involves an abuse of process, which would render its proceedings inefficacious, or which brings or tends to bring the administration of justice into disrepute.” [1998] HCA 9; (1998) 193 CLR 173 at [74].

In the result it is not an arbitrary exercise of power for the Court to weigh up competing factors and make a determination, even if, as is often the case, the task cannot be undertaken in a mathematical or precise manner. As the Court of Appeal found in Norris v Blake (by his Tutor Porter) [No 2] (1997) 41 NSWLR 49 mathematical weightings of potential outcomes in loss of chance cases might be unsustainable and reliance rather should be placed on more intuitive methods to determine loss: (1997) 41 NSWLR 49 at 71-73. Similarly, the courts are frequently required to estimate damages that are not capable of precise quantification and require a degree of approximation and even guesswork. [Commonwealth v Amann Aviation Pty Ltd [1991] HCA 54; (1991) 174 CLR 64 at 83, 138 and 153; Placer (Granny Smith) Pty Ltd v Thiess Contractors Pty Ltd [2003] HCA 10; (2003) 196 ALR 257 at [37]- [38]]

In Stoian & Fiening (Costs) [2014] FamCA 944, [Kent] J referred to those principles with approval. His Honour said:

In short, I am satisfied that the purpose of the subject rule to avoid the expense, delay and aggravation involved in further litigation surrounding assessment of costs ought in this case be achieved and is achievable.

I am fortified in the conclusion that, accepting that r 19.18(1)(a) is “to avoid the expense, delay and aggravation involved in protracted litigation arising out of taxation” that purpose or object ought be attained in any case where it is possible to so do by reference to the Rules more generally.

For example, r 1.04 expresses the main purpose of the Rules and provides as follows:

Rule 1.04 Main purpose of Rules

The main purpose of these Rules is to ensure that each case is resolved in a just and timely manner at a cost to the parties and the court that is reasonable in the circumstances of the case.

Rule 1.06 mandates that the Court applies the Rules to promote that main purpose.

99. Rule 1.07 relevantly provides:

Rule 1.07 Achieving the main purpose

To achieve the main purpose, the court applies these Rules in a way that:

(a) deals with each case fairly, justly and in a timely manner;

...

(c) is proportionate to the issues in a case and their complexity, and the likely costs of the case;

(d) promotes the saving of costs;

(e) gives an appropriate share of the court’s resources to a case, taking into account the needs of other cases; and

...

The first complaint is that the primary judge simply assumed the starting figure as to costs and that there was insufficient evidence to support it. It is doubtful that this submission falls within the ambit of the ground.

That evidence, such as it was, was the subject of limited criticism at the hearing before the primary judge. The husband complained that “those bills go back to 2021 ... [t]here’s a whole bunch of bills that are not relevant” (Transcript 18 July 2025, p.31 lines 1–2). His further submission was that most of the items on the Costs Notice “shouldn’t be included because they were part of the trial” (Transcript 18 July 2025, p.31 lines 10–11), though it is unclear what was meant by that.

The costs paid by the wife, or to be paid, may be taken as her solicitor/client costs in full. That is not an inappropriate place to start. The Costs Notice identified them as $566,216. His Honour proceeded on the lesser sum of $501,889 taken from the appeal judgment. It was consistent with the wife’s evidence that her legal costs were in excess of $500,000 (wife’s affidavit filed 17 February 2025, paragraph 22; wife’s affidavit filed 6 March 2025 at 6.15 pm, paragraph 8). That sum was then discounted by 40 to 50 per cent to allow for a reduction of those costs, which would not be an unexpected outcome following an assessment of those costs on a party and party basis at scale. There was thus a reasoned basis for the assessment of costs, which were then further discounted by assessing the portion to be paid by the husband.

It is true that there was an assumption that an assessment would result in a significant reduction in the costs, but that can hardly be controversial because that is the purpose and almost inevitable outcome of that process. It was a substantial reduction.

It is a broad-brush approach which accords with the above authorities.

The only authority referred to in the husband’s Summary of Argument under this ground was Sfakianakis v Sfakianakis [2019] FamCAFC 54; (2019) 59 Fam LR 419, which concerned the application of r 22.53(3) of the Family Law Rules 2004 (Cth) (now r 12.13(4) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”)). It requires an applicant for indemnity costs to adduce evidence of his or her costs agreements with their lawyers. The respondent sought an order for indemnity costs but did not comply with the rule. However, she failed in her claim for indemnity costs and the rule and authority on it is of no further relevance.

The written submissions identified just one entry in the Costs Notice in the sum of $4,000 and put the question - why was it allowed? The first answer is that it was not allowed in a specific sum. It was merely caught up in the general process discussed above. In one sense, then, it was discounted in that process.

The second answer is that it was not the subject of any submission to the primary judge.

It must be recalled that the husband was ordered to pay only 30 per cent of the wife’s costs as at 6 March 2025.

Finally, the very point of fixing costs in a lump sum is to avoid the line-by-line taxation of them.

This ground is not established.

Ground 2 – Did the primary judge fail to have proper regard to the scale of the costs?

It is submitted that the primary judge did not consider the scale of costs set out in Sch 3 of the Rules, particularly when assessing counsel’s fees as there was no evidence as to the basis on which they were charging.

Counsel’s fees, as set out in the Costs Notice, were of the order of $26,000 in total – a small fraction of the overall sum. The materiality of any error is questionable. In any event, in making a specific assessment the court is not bound by the scale, although it may be informed by it.

This ground does not succeed.

Ground 3 – Did the primary judge impermissibly use a ‘rule of thumb’?

This has been largely addressed under Ground 1, especially as the submissions repeated those made under that ground.

Ground 4 – Did the primary judge have regard to irrelevant findings?

At [60(g)] his Honour referred to adverse comments made by Campton J as to the conduct of an appeal by the husband that was before his Honour.

Section 114UB(3)(c) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) requires the court to consider “the conduct of the parties to the proceedings in relation to the proceedings” when determining what costs order, if any, should be made.

However, the court is also to take into account “such other matters as the court considers relevant” (s 114UB(3)(g) of the Act). This subsection permits the court to take into account conduct more generally, for example, as here, where the finding was that the poor conduct of the property proceedings was part of the husband’s overall conduct of the proceedings against the wife, which makes a costs order in these proceedings more justifiable.

This ground fails.

Ground 5 – Did the primary judge fail to determine whether or not the costs had been reasonably incurred?

This is the same territory as Grounds 1 and 2. It leads to the same result.

The purpose of a taxation or assessment is to try to identify reasonable costs. His Honour did so, not in a line-by-line analysis, but by identifying a gross sum. It is entirely unclear to me why that is not a way of excluding costs thought to be unreasonable.

Ground 6 – Did the primary judge fail to have regard to relevant matters being the failure to adduce adequate evidence and costs agreements?

This, again, is the same argument rebadged under a new ground. It does not enhance the attractiveness of the arguments already discussed and rejected.

Ground 7 – Did the primary judge give adequate reasons?

As identified at the outset, his Honour clearly explained his reasoning as to his quantification of the sum to be paid. It can easily be understood and has been the subject of detailed criticism.

The test for adequacy of reasons, as set out below, is well known (Bennett and Bennett [1990] FamCA 148; (1991) FLC 92-191 at 78,266). See also Pollard v RRR Corporation Pty Ltd [2009] NSWCA 110 at [57]–[59].

The adequacy of the reasons will depend upon the circumstances of the case. But the reasons will ... be inadequate if:–

(a) the appeal court is unable to ascertain the reasoning upon which the decision is based; or

(b) justice is not seen to have been done.

The two above stated criteria of inadequacy will frequently overlap. If the primary Judge does not sufficiently disclose his or her reasoning, the appeal court is denied the opportunity to detect error and the losing party is denied knowledge of why his or her case was rejected.

It follows that the reasons are adequate.

I would add that a failure of the reasons to refer to the wife bearing the burden of proof and r 12.13(4) of the Rules could hardly lead to inadequate reasons. A judge need not set out every applicable principle, especially where there is no dispute as to them. It was not suggested, for example, that the primary judge erred on the onus of proof.

Disposition

It follows that the appeal fails and will be dismissed.

The cross-appeal

The wife cross-appealed against the costs order, her grounds being:

Ground 1 – Denial of Procedural Fairness (Costs)

The primary judge denied the Wife procedural fairness by:

(a) advancing arguments on behalf of the Husband in circumstances where he filed no affidavit and no submissions in response to the costs application;

(b) relying on matters adverse to the Wife without affording her a proper or considered opportunity to respond and correct the record;

(c) failing to accept, or impermissibly displacing, the Wife’s uncontested sworn affidavit evidence (including evidence addressing [the N Street property] and related property issues), and reducing the costs outcome on a factual premise contrary to that evidence ; and

(d) reducing the costs outcome on grounds that were never reasonably or fairly put to the Wife, whether in writing or in a manner that afforded her a meaningful opportunity to address them.

Ground 2 – Miscarriage of Discretion in Costs

The primary judge miscarried the discretion under s 117 of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) by failing to award indemnity costs despite findings of systemic dishonesty, non-disclosure, contempt, and misleading conduct by the Husband.

(Amended Notice of Cross-Appeal filed 13 February 2026)

Most unhelpfully, the Summary of Argument did not address either of the grounds directly.

Her primary submission was that, on the facts, “[i]ndemnity costs were therefore required, not discretionary” (wife’s Summary of Argument filed 13 February 2026, paragraph 7).

That is an entirely incorrect statement of the law. Costs, including indemnity costs, involves the exercise of a discretion under s 114UB of the Act.

To the extent the submission is that, given the husband’s conduct, the outcome is plainly wrong or unreasonable, the obvious answer is that the unchallenged findings as to the conduct of the wife would preclude such a conclusion.

Finally, the application for indemnity costs was doomed to fail as a result of a failure to comply with r 12.13(4) of the Rules.

It was then submitted that there was a denial procedural fairness because:

16. ... the trial judge:

(a) advanced arguments on the Husband’s behalf;

(b) reduced the costs quantum; and

(c) relied on matters never put to the Wife.

(Wife’s Summary of Argument filed 13 February 2026)

The primary judge was obliged to consider all matters raised by s 114UB as he considered relevant, even if not raised by the wife. The wife did not deign to identify the specific arguments or matters in either her written or oral submissions that she takes issue with. It is not for the court to guess what those are. It is impossible to take these submissions further.

His Honour, when fixing an amount for costs, even indemnity costs, is not bound to accept the costs claimed simply on the say so of the wife. Even indemnity costs must be reasonably incurred.

The wife submitted that the primary judge erred by finding that the wife had not made a settlement offer [at [60(f)]). She did not point to any evidence of any offer so as to demonstrate the error, if any.

The cross-appeal against the costs order fails.

The wife also appealed against the following enforcement orders:

3. With respect to the Husband’s Application – Enforcement filed 10 April 2025:

(a) The Wife shall advise the Husband in writing on or before 24 October 2025, whether her refinancing of and discharge of the Husband’s liability under Westpac loan #...99, shall require the sale of either [B Street, Suburb C,] Brisbane or [N Street,] Brisbane. If one of those properties is to be sold, the Wife shall nominate the property.

(b) The Wife shall do all acts and sign all documents prior to 31 October 2025, to appoint a real estate agent to list the nominated property for sale and shall keep the Husband advised with respect to all actions taken by her to facilitate the sale.

(c) Whether or not a real property of the Wife is to be sold, Westpac loan #...99 shall be refinanced by the Wife and the Husband’s liability under that loan shall be discharged, on or before 10 January 2026.

The original property orders provided for the wife to receive the matrimonial home but to discharge a mortgage over it so that the obligation of the husband under that mortgage was discharged. At the time of the enforcement hearing, the mortgage had not been re-financed.

In support of these grounds, the wife relied heavily on the evidence she seeks to adduce on the appeal. She submitted that it “undermined the integrity” of the enforcement orders.

In short, in late 2025, the police charged the husband with forging the wife’s signature on two home loans with Westpac Bank, as well as other charges of forgery and uttering.

The judgment in the primary property proceedings (Poirier & Poirier [2024] FedCFamC2F 559) reveals that the wife owned a property at Suburb C with a value of $1.4 million. It was subject to a loan from Westpac in the sum of $421,674 with the liability described as joint. Presumably this is the loan the subject of at least one of the forgery charges.

The wife raised the possibility of the criminal charges at the enforcement hearing. As the primary judge pointed out in his reasons, he could not vary the final orders on such an application. He had to deal with the orders as they stood. In any event, the evidence as it stood at the time would not have justified such a course.

If the husband did indeed forge the wife’s signature on the loan application, and presumably the accompanying mortgage over the property, then there is an arguable case that they should both be set aside. That, of course, would be subject to the bank arguing that it has acted reasonably and had no reason to suspect any forgery or improper dealing. If the wife’s claim against the bank succeeded, the Suburb C property would be unencumbered in the hands of the wife but the bank would presumably retain its claim against the husband. In such a case it would be difficult to see why the wife should make any payment to the bank for the benefit of the husband.

Obviously, the police case is at a very early stage. I was not provided with any evidence as to the asserted forgery – I was only given details of the charges. However, it remains a possibility that the agreements between the wife and Westpac will be set aside. In the light of such a possibility, it is difficult to see how enforcement orders for the sale of the property, for the benefit of the husband, could properly be made until the outcome of the that process is known. It would be unfair to require the wife to sell a property to meet a claim that could well be set aside.

It is in the interests of justice to admit the further evidence, despite it being general in form. That evidence makes it difficult to see that the immediate forced sale of property is reasonable. There was a real possibility that such evidence, had it then been available would have at least resulted in an adjournment for the purpose of obtaining more detailed material from the police.

The cross-appeal will be allowed in part, Order 3 will be set aside and the enforcement application remitted for re-hearing on what will hopefully be much more extensive evidence.

The wife should receive the costs of filing the cross-appeal. It is justified by its success.

sc.zh: 费用——各自承担为原则，法院认为公正时可命令付费
sc.en: Costs — parties bear their own unless the court considers an order just

desc.zh: 费用（含赔偿性费用）属酌量事项：当事人原则上各自承担费用，除非法院认为命令公正。法院须考量 s 114UB(3) 所列事项，包括 (3)(c) 当事人在程序中的行为，以及 (3)(g) 法院认为相关的其他事项。
desc.en: Costs (including indemnity costs) are a matter of discretion: parties bear their own unless the court considers an order just. The court must take into account the matters in s 114UB(3), including (3)(c) the conduct of the parties in relation to the proceedings, and (3)(g) such other matters as the court considers relevant.

t.en: Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) — r 12.13, Sch 3

sc.zh: 申请赔偿性费用须提交费用协议证据；附表 3 为费用表
sc.en: Indemnity costs require evidence of costs agreements; Sch 3 is the scale

desc.zh: r 12.13(4) 要求申请赔偿性费用的一方提交其与律师的费用协议证据；未遵守者，赔偿性费用申请注定失败。附表 3（Sch 3）载明费用表——法院酌定费用时可参考，但不受其拘束。
desc.en: Rule 12.13(4) requires a party seeking indemnity costs to adduce evidence of their costs agreements with their lawyers; non-compliance dooms an indemnity-costs application. Sch 3 sets out the scale — which may inform, but does not bind, the court when making a specific assessment.

summary.zh: 确立说理充分性（adequacy of reasons）的检验标准的经典权威。
summary.en: The well-known authority setting out the test for the adequacy of reasons.

prop.zh: 若上诉法院无法查明裁决所依据的说理，或正义未彰显，则说理不充分；说理不足会剥夺上诉法院察觉错误的机会，并使败诉方无从知悉败诉之由。
prop.en: Reasons are inadequate if the appeal court cannot ascertain the reasoning on which the decision is based, or if justice is not seen to be done; inadequate reasons deny the appeal court the chance to detect error and the losing party knowledge of why they lost.

summary.zh: 本案系当事人间此前的上诉判决（Campton J）。初审法官取用的费用起算数额（501,889 澳元）即源自该判决第 [90] 段。
summary.en: An earlier appeal in the same litigation (Campton J). The primary judge's starting costs figure ($501,889) was taken from paragraph [90] of that judgment.

prop.zh: 为本案费用酌定提供了女方实际费用数额的事实基础。
prop.en: Provided the factual basis (the wife's actual costs figure) for the costs assessment in this matter.

summary.zh: 本案的主财产诉讼判决。其中载明女方在 Suburb C 拥有价值 140 万澳元的房产，负有西太平洋银行（Westpac）421,674 澳元的贷款（描述为共同负债）。
summary.en: The judgment in the principal property proceedings. It records that the wife owned a Suburb C property valued at $1.4 million, subject to a Westpac loan of $421,674 described as joint.

prop.zh: 提供了强制执行命令所涉房产与按揭的背景事实；该按揭据信即涉嫌伪造指控之标的。
prop.en: Provided the background facts as to the property and mortgage the subject of the enforcement orders; that mortgage is presumably the subject of the forgery charges.

t.zh: Pollard v RRR Corporation Pty Ltd
t.en: Pollard v RRR Corporation Pty Ltd

court.zh: 新南威尔士州上诉法院
court.en: New South Wales Court of Appeal

summary.zh: 就说理充分性所引的辅助权威（与 Bennett 并列）。
summary.en: A supporting authority on the adequacy of reasons (cited alongside Bennett).

prop.zh: 补充说明判决说理是否充分的判断标准。
prop.en: Supplements the test for whether reasons for judgment are adequate.

summary.zh: 涉及申请赔偿性费用须提交费用协议证据之规则（旧 r 22.53(3)，现为 r 12.13(4)）。
summary.en: Concerns the rule requiring an applicant for indemnity costs to adduce evidence of their costs agreements (former r 22.53(3), now r 12.13(4)).

prop.zh: 申请赔偿性费用者须遵守提交费用协议证据的规则；未遵守则该申请失败。本案中因女方失败于赔偿性费用主张，该权威于此已无进一步意义。
prop.en: An applicant for indemnity costs must comply with the rule to adduce costs-agreement evidence; failure defeats the claim. Here, as the wife failed on indemnity costs, the rule and authority were of no further relevance.

summary.zh: Aldridge J 本人此前的判决，就以「大笔酌定」方式定出一笔固定费用金额的原则作了总结。
summary.en: Aldridge J's own earlier judgment, summarising the principles for fixing a gross sum of costs on a broad-brush basis.

prop.zh: 法律与规则明确允许法院「定出」应付费用的金额；固定费用的目的在于避免因逐项评定所致的费用、拖延与困扰。评定必然涉及「大笔酌定」，须合乎逻辑、公平、合理。
prop.en: The Act and Rules clearly permit the court to fix the amount of costs; the purpose of fixing costs is to avoid the expense, delay and aggravation of a line-by-line taxation. The assessment necessarily involves a broad brush and must be logical, fair and reasonable.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

ALDRIDGE J:

On 10 October 2025 a judge of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 2) made costs and enforcement orders in relation to completed property proceedings. Mr Poirier (“the husband”) has appealed the costs orders. Ms Poirier (“the wife”) has cross-appealed the costs order and also challenges the enforcement orders made against her.

The appeal

The primary judge ordered the husband to pay the wife’s costs of the principal proceedings in the sum of $150,000, on or before 14 November 2025.
The essential reasons for making the costs order were summarised by his Honour as follows:
My view was and remains, that the Husband:
(a) Did not provide timely disclosure of his financial interests.

(b) Did not provide complete disclosure of his financial interests.

(c) Was not honest about his financial interests.
(d) As a result of (a) – (c) made it impossible or extremely difficult to value his financial interests.


(e) Was not honest in cross-examination during the trial.
(f) Pressed his father [Mr T] to give dishonest evidence in the trial.
In my view, the Husband’s actions increased considerably the Wife’s legal costs. Her lawyers had to examine in detail the financial documents the Husband did produce. This included trying to ascertain what documents (and there appear to have been many) were missing. Faced with insufficient disclosure, the Wife’s solicitors were required to issue multiple subpoenas and investigate matters which should have been obvious had the Husband given full and frank disclosure.
The primary judge found that the conduct of the proceedings by the wife was “less than perfect and indeed, less than required by the Act and the Rules” (at [60(c)]). However, it was found that the wife incurred considerably greater costs than she should have as a result of the husband’s conduct.
As to quantum, the wife’s actual legal costs were $501,889. His Honour went on to say, assessed on an ordinary basis, some 50 to 60 per cent of this would be allowed (i.e. $250,000 to $300,000).
The primary judge determined that the husband “should pay a reasonably high amount of what I estimate to be the party and party costs of the principal proceedings” (at [68]). He thus fixed the costs payable at $150,000.
The husband raises seven grounds of appeal which were, unfortunately, somewhat repetitive and overlapping.

Ground 1 – Did the primary judge fail to exercise discretion in a manner which was consistent with authority, logical, fair and reasonable?

The primary judge’s reasoning, briefly expressed, was:
The wife placed into evidence an affidavit filed 6 March 2025 (at 6.15 pm, as there appear to be two) which annexed a “Costs Notice” for a hearing on 10 March 2025, which showed her costs at that time to be $566,216.
His Honour took the relevant costs to be $501,889 as the amount actually paid by the wife, assumed they were her total costs, and assumed that taxation on a party and party basis would yield a result of 50 to 60 per cent (or $250,000 to $300,000) (at [64]). It appears that the first of these figures was taken from the reasons of Campton J on appeal (Poirier & Poirier (No 2) [2024] FedCFamC1A 231 at [90]).
The husband should pay a reasonably large proportion of that percentage.
The approach to assessing and fixing costs is well known. It necessarily involves a broad brush. In Sresbodan & Sresbodan [2016] FamCA 954, I said (in relation to s 117 as the section then was):
Section 117 of the Act and r 19.18(1)(a) of the Family Law Rules 2004 (Cth) clearly permit the Court to fix the amount of the costs to be paid.
Whilst it is relatively common for costs to be fixed where the costs are not significant, it is rare, for obvious reasons, where the costs are substantial.
In Idoport Pty Limited v National Australia Bank Limited and Ors, Idoport Pty Limited v Donald Robert Argus [2007] NSWSC 23, Einstein J considered the application of s 98(4)(c) of the Civil Procedure Act NSW (2005), which permits the making of an order for costs in “a specified gross sum”. There the costs sought exceeded $61 million. Einstein J comprehensively summarised the principles to be involved in such an assessment, saying:
As may be expected in what is likely the largest claim ever made in this country for a gross sum costs order, both parties took the court to the authorities which inform the principled exercise of the relevant discretion. Notwithstanding that some areas of difference arose concerning disparate parameters/appropriate emphases, in terms of the application of the appropriate principles, there were in the main no areas of serious disagreement.
For present purposes it seems convenient to commence with a recitation of the principles which inform the exercise of the discretion:
the purpose of the rule is to avoid the expense, delay and aggravation involved in protracted litigation arising out of taxation: Beach Petroleum NL v Johnson (1995) 57 FCR 119, Von Doussa J page 265: [following Purchase J in Leary v Leary [1987] 1 All ER 261 who described the purpose of the rule allowing the fixing of a gross sum as “the avoidance of expense, delay and aggravation involved in protracted litigation arising out of taxation” (All ER page 265)];
the touchstone requires that the Court be confident that the approach taken to estimate costs is logical, fair and reasonable: Beach Petroleum at [16];
the fairness parameter includes the Court having sufficient confidence in arriving at an appropriate sum on the materials available: Harrison v Schipp [2002] NSWCA 213; (2002) 54 NSWLR 738, per Giles JA at para [22]; [following (Wentworth v Wentworth (CA, 21 February 1996, unreported, per Clarke JA) and adopted in Sony Entertainment v Smith (2005) 215 ALR 788; [2005] FCA 228; BC200500963 at para [199];
a gross sum assessment, by its very nature, does not envisage that a process similar to that involved in a traditional taxation or assessment of costs should take place: Harrison v Schipp at para [22];
the gross sum “can only be fixed broadly having regard to the information before the Court”: Beach Petroleum at 124;
[In Hadid v Lenfest Communications Inc [2000] FCA 628 at [35] it was said that the evidence enabled fixing a gross sum “only if I apply a much broader brush than would be applied on taxation, but that ... is what the rule contemplates”.]
nevertheless the power to award a gross sum must be exercised judicially, and after giving the parties an adequate opportunity to make submissions on the matter: Leary v Leary [1987] 1 WLR 72 at 76, and Beach Petroleum NL v Johnson (No.2) (1995) 57 FCR 119 at 120;
In terms of the necessity for the approach taken to be logical, fair and reasonable, Von Doussa J in Beach Petroleum NL & Anor v Johnson & Ors (No 2) (1995) 57 FCR 119, put the matter as follows, at paras [16]:


“On the one hand the Court must be astute to prevent prejudice to the respondents by overestimating the costs, and on the other hand must be astute not to cause an injustice to the successful party by an arbitrary ‘fail safe’ discount on the cost estimates submitted to the Court: Leary v Leary at 265. ... ”
A closer examination of the requirement to act judicially
I accept as correct the submissions of the Idoport Parties in contending that the proper approach of the Court in the present environment should not be described as ‘arbitrary’. In short:
although the principles set forth above are helpful in providing guidance to the Court as to the manner in which the discretion to make a gross sum costs order is to be exercised, ultimately whether an approach is logical, fair and reasonable falls to be determined by reference to the particular case before the Court;
as explained by Purchas LJ in Leary v Leary [1987] 1 WLR 72 at 76:
“The unlimited discretion given by Ord. 62, r. 9 must be exercised in a judicial manner. How the powers are to be used varies widely from case to case and each case must be considered on its own merits. It is easy to envisage cases where a judge could be said to have acted unjudicially, e.g. by clutching a figure out of the air without having any indication as to the estimated costs; receiving such an estimate without the details being made available to the other side; or refusing a request to hear submissions on such a schedule if the party against whom the order is to be made makes, on reasonable grounds, an application to be heard.”;
the requirement that the power to award a gross sum should be exercised judicially does not mean that it must be exercised in any scientific or formulaic manner.; At the heart of the judicial function is the responsibility to weigh up competing factors and considerations, many of which might conflict, to reach a determination. Acting judicially carries with it an obligation to apply the rules of natural justice, to act impartially and to apply the law to the facts. As Kitto J explained in The Queen v Trade Practices Commission; Ex parte Tasmanian Breweries Pty Ltd [1970] HCA 8; (1970) 123 CLR 361 at 374:


“Thus a judicial power involves, as a general rule, a decision settling for the future, as between defined persons or classes of persons, a question as to the existence of a right or obligation so that an exercise of the power creates a new charter by reference to which that question is in the future to be decided as between those persons or classes of persons. In other words, the process to be followed must generally be an inquiry concerning the law as it is and the facts as they are, followed by an application of the law as determined to the facts as determined; and the end to be reached must be an act which, so long as it stands, entitles and obliges the persons between whom it intervenes, to observance of the rights and obligations that the application of law to facts has shown to exist.”;
the scope and nature of judicial power was also addressed by Gaudron J in Harris v Caladine [1991] HCA 9; (1991) 172 CLR 84 at 150 in these terms:


“Judicial power is usually defined in terms of its subject matter, but it is a power that, for complete definition, requires description of its dominant and essential characteristic, namely, that it is exercised in accordance with that process which is referred to as ‘the judicial process’. Thus, in general terms, it is a power which cannot be exercised until the ‘tribunal which has power ... is called upon to take action’ (Huddart Parker), which (subject to limited exceptions) proceeds by way of open and public inquiry, which involves the application of the rules of natural justice, and which is directed to ascertaining ‘the law as it is and the facts as they are, followed by an application of the law as determined to the facts as determined’: Tasmanian Breweries, per Kitto J.” (references omitted);
more recently, Gaudron J explained the nature of judicial power in Nicholas v The Queen in these terms:


“In my view, consistency with the essential character of a court and with the nature of judicial power necessitates that a court not be required or authorised to proceed in a manner that does not ensure equality before the law, impartiality and the appearance of impartiality, the right of a party to meet the case made against him or her, the independent determination of the matter in controversy by application of the law to facts determined in accordance with the rules and procedures which truly permit the facts to be ascertained and, in the case of criminal proceedings, the determination of guilt or innocence by means of a fair trial according to the law. It means, moreover, that a court cannot be required or authorised to proceed in any manner which involves an abuse of process, which would render its proceedings inefficacious, or which brings or tends to bring the administration of justice into disrepute.” [1998] HCA 9; (1998) 193 CLR 173 at [74].
In the result it is not an arbitrary exercise of power for the Court to weigh up competing factors and make a determination, even if, as is often the case, the task cannot be undertaken in a mathematical or precise manner. As the Court of Appeal found in Norris v Blake (by his Tutor Porter) [No 2] (1997) 41 NSWLR 49 mathematical weightings of potential outcomes in loss of chance cases might be unsustainable and reliance rather should be placed on more intuitive methods to determine loss: (1997) 41 NSWLR 49 at 71-73. Similarly, the courts are frequently required to estimate damages that are not capable of precise quantification and require a degree of approximation and even guesswork. [Commonwealth v Amann Aviation Pty Ltd [1991] HCA 54; (1991) 174 CLR 64 at 83, 138 and 153; Placer (Granny Smith) Pty Ltd v Thiess Contractors Pty Ltd [2003] HCA 10; (2003) 196 ALR 257 at [37]- [38]]
In Stoian & Fiening (Costs) [2014] FamCA 944, [Kent] J referred to those principles with approval. His Honour said:
In short, I am satisfied that the purpose of the subject rule to avoid the expense, delay and aggravation involved in further litigation surrounding assessment of costs ought in this case be achieved and is achievable.
I am fortified in the conclusion that, accepting that r 19.18(1)(a) is “to avoid the expense, delay and aggravation involved in protracted litigation arising out of taxation” that purpose or object ought be attained in any case where it is possible to so do by reference to the Rules more generally.
For example, r 1.04 expresses the main purpose of the Rules and provides as follows:
Rule 1.04 Main purpose of Rules

The main purpose of these Rules is to ensure that each case is resolved in a just and timely manner at a cost to the parties and the court that is reasonable in the circumstances of the case.
Rule 1.06 mandates that the Court applies the Rules to promote that main purpose.
99. Rule 1.07 relevantly provides:
Rule 1.07 Achieving the main purpose

To achieve the main purpose, the court applies these Rules in a way that:

(a) deals with each case fairly, justly and in a timely manner;

...
(c) is proportionate to the issues in a case and their complexity, and the likely costs of the case;


(d) promotes the saving of costs;
(e) gives an appropriate share of the court’s resources to a case, taking into account the needs of other cases; and


...
The first complaint is that the primary judge simply assumed the starting figure as to costs and that there was insufficient evidence to support it. It is doubtful that this submission falls within the ambit of the ground.
That evidence, such as it was, was the subject of limited criticism at the hearing before the primary judge. The husband complained that “those bills go back to 2021 ... [t]here’s a whole bunch of bills that are not relevant” (Transcript 18 July 2025, p.31 lines 1–2). His further submission was that most of the items on the Costs Notice “shouldn’t be included because they were part of the trial” (Transcript 18 July 2025, p.31 lines 10–11), though it is unclear what was meant by that.
The costs paid by the wife, or to be paid, may be taken as her solicitor/client costs in full. That is not an inappropriate place to start. The Costs Notice identified them as $566,216. His Honour proceeded on the lesser sum of $501,889 taken from the appeal judgment. It was consistent with the wife’s evidence that her legal costs were in excess of $500,000 (wife’s affidavit filed 17 February 2025, paragraph 22; wife’s affidavit filed 6 March 2025 at 6.15 pm, paragraph 8). That sum was then discounted by 40 to 50 per cent to allow for a reduction of those costs, which would not be an unexpected outcome following an assessment of those costs on a party and party basis at scale. There was thus a reasoned basis for the assessment of costs, which were then further discounted by assessing the portion to be paid by the husband.
It is true that there was an assumption that an assessment would result in a significant reduction in the costs, but that can hardly be controversial because that is the purpose and almost inevitable outcome of that process. It was a substantial reduction.
It is a broad-brush approach which accords with the above authorities.
The only authority referred to in the husband’s Summary of Argument under this ground was Sfakianakis v Sfakianakis [2019] FamCAFC 54; (2019) 59 Fam LR 419, which concerned the application of r 22.53(3) of the Family Law Rules 2004 (Cth) (now r 12.13(4) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”)). It requires an applicant for indemnity costs to adduce evidence of his or her costs agreements with their lawyers. The respondent sought an order for indemnity costs but did not comply with the rule. However, she failed in her claim for indemnity costs and the rule and authority on it is of no further relevance.
The written submissions identified just one entry in the Costs Notice in the sum of $4,000 and put the question - why was it allowed? The first answer is that it was not allowed in a specific sum. It was merely caught up in the general process discussed above. In one sense, then, it was discounted in that process.
The second answer is that it was not the subject of any submission to the primary judge.
It must be recalled that the husband was ordered to pay only 30 per cent of the wife’s costs as at 6 March 2025.
Finally, the very point of fixing costs in a lump sum is to avoid the line-by-line taxation of them.
This ground is not established.

Ground 2 – Did the primary judge fail to have proper regard to the scale of the costs?

It is submitted that the primary judge did not consider the scale of costs set out in Sch 3 of the Rules, particularly when assessing counsel’s fees as there was no evidence as to the basis on which they were charging.
Counsel’s fees, as set out in the Costs Notice, were of the order of $26,000 in total – a small fraction of the overall sum. The materiality of any error is questionable. In any event, in making a specific assessment the court is not bound by the scale, although it may be informed by it.
This ground does not succeed.

Ground 3 – Did the primary judge impermissibly use a ‘rule of thumb’?

This has been largely addressed under Ground 1, especially as the submissions repeated those made under that ground.

Ground 4 – Did the primary judge have regard to irrelevant findings?

At [60(g)] his Honour referred to adverse comments made by Campton J as to the conduct of an appeal by the husband that was before his Honour.
Section 114UB(3)(c) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) requires the court to consider “the conduct of the parties to the proceedings in relation to the proceedings” when determining what costs order, if any, should be made.
However, the court is also to take into account “such other matters as the court considers relevant” (s 114UB(3)(g) of the Act). This subsection permits the court to take into account conduct more generally, for example, as here, where the finding was that the poor conduct of the property proceedings was part of the husband’s overall conduct of the proceedings against the wife, which makes a costs order in these proceedings more justifiable.
This ground fails.

Ground 5 – Did the primary judge fail to determine whether or not the costs had been reasonably incurred?

This is the same territory as Grounds 1 and 2. It leads to the same result.
The purpose of a taxation or assessment is to try to identify reasonable costs. His Honour did so, not in a line-by-line analysis, but by identifying a gross sum. It is entirely unclear to me why that is not a way of excluding costs thought to be unreasonable.

Ground 6 – Did the primary judge fail to have regard to relevant matters being the failure to adduce adequate evidence and costs agreements?

This, again, is the same argument rebadged under a new ground. It does not enhance the attractiveness of the arguments already discussed and rejected.

Ground 7 – Did the primary judge give adequate reasons?

As identified at the outset, his Honour clearly explained his reasoning as to his quantification of the sum to be paid. It can easily be understood and has been the subject of detailed criticism.
The test for adequacy of reasons, as set out below, is well known (Bennett and Bennett [1990] FamCA 148; (1991) FLC 92-191 at 78,266). See also Pollard v RRR Corporation Pty Ltd [2009] NSWCA 110 at [57]–[59].
The adequacy of the reasons will depend upon the circumstances of the case. But the reasons will ... be inadequate if:–
(a) the appeal court is unable to ascertain the reasoning upon which the decision is based; or
(b) justice is not seen to have been done.
The two above stated criteria of inadequacy will frequently overlap. If the primary Judge does not sufficiently disclose his or her reasoning, the appeal court is denied the opportunity to detect error and the losing party is denied knowledge of why his or her case was rejected.
It follows that the reasons are adequate.
I would add that a failure of the reasons to refer to the wife bearing the burden of proof and r 12.13(4) of the Rules could hardly lead to inadequate reasons. A judge need not set out every applicable principle, especially where there is no dispute as to them. It was not suggested, for example, that the primary judge erred on the onus of proof.

Disposition

It follows that the appeal fails and will be dismissed.

The cross-appeal

The wife cross-appealed against the costs order, her grounds being:
Ground 1 – Denial of Procedural Fairness (Costs)

The primary judge denied the Wife procedural fairness by:
(a) advancing arguments on behalf of the Husband in circumstances where he filed no affidavit and no submissions in response to the costs application;

(b) relying on matters adverse to the Wife without affording her a proper or considered opportunity to respond and correct the record;

(c) failing to accept, or impermissibly displacing, the Wife’s uncontested sworn affidavit evidence (including evidence addressing [the N Street property] and related property issues), and reducing the costs outcome on a factual premise contrary to that evidence ; and

(d) reducing the costs outcome on grounds that were never reasonably or fairly put to the Wife, whether in writing or in a manner that afforded her a meaningful opportunity to address them.

Ground 2 – Miscarriage of Discretion in Costs

The primary judge miscarried the discretion under s 117 of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) by failing to award indemnity costs despite findings of systemic dishonesty, non-disclosure, contempt, and misleading conduct by the Husband.

(Amended Notice of Cross-Appeal filed 13 February 2026)
Most unhelpfully, the Summary of Argument did not address either of the grounds directly.
Her primary submission was that, on the facts, “[i]ndemnity costs were therefore required, not discretionary” (wife’s Summary of Argument filed 13 February 2026, paragraph 7).
That is an entirely incorrect statement of the law. Costs, including indemnity costs, involves the exercise of a discretion under s 114UB of the Act.
To the extent the submission is that, given the husband’s conduct, the outcome is plainly wrong or unreasonable, the obvious answer is that the unchallenged findings as to the conduct of the wife would preclude such a conclusion.
Finally, the application for indemnity costs was doomed to fail as a result of a failure to comply with r 12.13(4) of the Rules.
It was then submitted that there was a denial procedural fairness because:
16. ... the trial judge:
(a) advanced arguments on the Husband’s behalf;

(b) reduced the costs quantum; and

(c) relied on matters never put to the Wife.
(Wife’s Summary of Argument filed 13 February 2026)
The primary judge was obliged to consider all matters raised by s 114UB as he considered relevant, even if not raised by the wife. The wife did not deign to identify the specific arguments or matters in either her written or oral submissions that she takes issue with. It is not for the court to guess what those are. It is impossible to take these submissions further.
His Honour, when fixing an amount for costs, even indemnity costs, is not bound to accept the costs claimed simply on the say so of the wife. Even indemnity costs must be reasonably incurred.
The wife submitted that the primary judge erred by finding that the wife had not made a settlement offer [at [60(f)]). She did not point to any evidence of any offer so as to demonstrate the error, if any.
The cross-appeal against the costs order fails.
The wife also appealed against the following enforcement orders:
3. With respect to the Husband’s Application – Enforcement filed 10 April 2025:
(a) The Wife shall advise the Husband in writing on or before 24 October 2025, whether her refinancing of and discharge of the Husband’s liability under Westpac loan #...99, shall require the sale of either [B Street, Suburb C,] Brisbane or [N Street,] Brisbane. If one of those properties is to be sold, the Wife shall nominate the property.

(b) The Wife shall do all acts and sign all documents prior to 31 October 2025, to appoint a real estate agent to list the nominated property for sale and shall keep the Husband advised with respect to all actions taken by her to facilitate the sale.

(c) Whether or not a real property of the Wife is to be sold, Westpac loan #...99 shall be refinanced by the Wife and the Husband’s liability under that loan shall be discharged, on or before 10 January 2026.

The original property orders provided for the wife to receive the matrimonial home but to discharge a mortgage over it so that the obligation of the husband under that mortgage was discharged. At the time of the enforcement hearing, the mortgage had not been re-financed.
In support of these grounds, the wife relied heavily on the evidence she seeks to adduce on the appeal. She submitted that it “undermined the integrity” of the enforcement orders.
In short, in late 2025, the police charged the husband with forging the wife’s signature on two home loans with Westpac Bank, as well as other charges of forgery and uttering.
The judgment in the primary property proceedings (Poirier & Poirier [2024] FedCFamC2F 559) reveals that the wife owned a property at Suburb C with a value of $1.4 million. It was subject to a loan from Westpac in the sum of $421,674 with the liability described as joint. Presumably this is the loan the subject of at least one of the forgery charges.
The wife raised the possibility of the criminal charges at the enforcement hearing. As the primary judge pointed out in his reasons, he could not vary the final orders on such an application. He had to deal with the orders as they stood. In any event, the evidence as it stood at the time would not have justified such a course.
If the husband did indeed forge the wife’s signature on the loan application, and presumably the accompanying mortgage over the property, then there is an arguable case that they should both be set aside. That, of course, would be subject to the bank arguing that it has acted reasonably and had no reason to suspect any forgery or improper dealing. If the wife’s claim against the bank succeeded, the Suburb C property would be unencumbered in the hands of the wife but the bank would presumably retain its claim against the husband. In such a case it would be difficult to see why the wife should make any payment to the bank for the benefit of the husband.
Obviously, the police case is at a very early stage. I was not provided with any evidence as to the asserted forgery – I was only given details of the charges. However, it remains a possibility that the agreements between the wife and Westpac will be set aside. In the light of such a possibility, it is difficult to see how enforcement orders for the sale of the property, for the benefit of the husband, could properly be made until the outcome of the that process is known. It would be unfair to require the wife to sell a property to meet a claim that could well be set aside.
It is in the interests of justice to admit the further evidence, despite it being general in form. That evidence makes it difficult to see that the immediate forced sale of property is reasonable. There was a real possibility that such evidence, had it then been available would have at least resulted in an adjournment for the purpose of obtaining more detailed material from the police.
The cross-appeal will be allowed in part, Order 3 will be set aside and the enforcement application remitted for re-hearing on what will hopefully be much more extensive evidence.
The wife should receive the costs of filing the cross-appeal. It is justified by its success.
